summary: Jeff Atwood explores the emerging technology of external GPU enclosures via Thunderbolt 3, using his own $500 Razer Core setup to power 4K gaming on a compact NUC system. He argues that while current costs are prohibitive, the technology represents a fundamental shift in how we think about computer upgradeability. The post details the technical specifications, practical limitations, and setup quirks of external GPUs while making the case that Thunderbolt 3's 40Gbps bandwidth is sufficient for high-end graphics cards with only modest performance penalties. Atwood envisions a future where any device with a Thunderbolt 3 port can transform into a gaming powerhouse, democratizing GPU upgrades for laptops and compact systems alike.
key_points: Thunderbolt 3 provides 40Gbps bandwidth (4.8 GB/s), enough for external GPUs with only 15-25% performance overhead
Modern CPUs are so fast that even budget processors won't bottleneck gaming - the GPU is almost always the limiting factor
External GPU enclosures offer a path to upgradeability for laptops and compact systems that traditionally couldn't be upgraded
Current costs ($500 for enclosure alone) need to drop dramatically for mainstream adoption
The technology works today but requires navigating quirks like cable length limits, multi-GPU management, and enclosure compatibility
money_quotes: I paid $500 for an external Thunderbolt 3 enclosure to fit a $600 video card, all to enable a plug-in upgrade of a GPU on a Skull Canyon NUC that itself cost around $1000 fully built. I know, it sounds crazy, and… OK fine, I won't argue with you. It's crazy.
Plain old regular HD, aka 1080p, aka 1920 × 1080, is one quarter the size of 4k, and ¼ the work. By today's GPU standards HD is pretty much easy mode these days. It's not even interesting.
It's so good that I'm now a little angry that every display that my eyes touch isn't OLED already.
The CPU and memory speeds available today are so comically fast that any device with a low-end i3-7100 for $120 will make zero difference in real world gaming at 1080p or higher.
Almost any small device you can buy with a Thunderbolt 3 port can now magically transform into a potent gaming rig with a single plug.
The future, as they say, is already here – it's just not evenly distributed.
I am painfully aware that costs need to come down. Way, way down.
key_insight: Thunderbolt 3 external GPU enclosures represent a paradigm shift toward modular, upgradeable computing that could finally free laptops and compact systems from their traditional graphics limitations.
